findings.title: Transient Balancer `exitPool` state can inflate Balancer-LP collateral prices and bypass collateral-removal solvency checks

best_agent_match.title: Transient Balancer-exit pricing window likely also enables over-borrowing, not only collateral removal

findings.title: The same transient Balancer-exit pricing window likely also permits direct over-borrowing during the callback

best_agent_match.title: Transient Balancer-exit pricing window likely also enables over-borrowing, not only collateral removal

rejected_candidates.title: Debtors can self-liquidate and internalize the liquidation bonus
rejected_candidates.reason: The code only shows self-liquidation being used after the primary oracle-manipulation exploit has already made the position unhealthy; it is not shown to create independent protocol loss, and on its own it mostly changes who captures the liquidation bonus rather than harming the pool.

rejected_candidates.title: ERC20 helper libraries accept EOAs and non-contract addresses as successful token operations
rejected_candidates.reason: This is a generic helper-library caveat, but the in-scope code does not show any protocol flow where token addresses are user-controlled or misconfigured, so no concrete exploitable path is established here.

rejected_candidates.title: safeApprove wrappers preserve the ERC20 allowance race condition
rejected_candidates.reason: This is the standard ERC20 approval caveat in generic helper code; no in-scope protocol flow demonstrates a non-zero-to-non-zero allowance change to an adversarial spender that would make it a distinct reportable issue.

rejected_candidates.title: ETH helper functions forward all gas and turn payouts into reentrancy gadgets
rejected_candidates.reason: Forwarding gas during ETH sends is only a reentrancy primitive, not a vulnerability by itself, and the provided code does not show an unsafe state-changing caller using these helpers in a way that creates a separate exploitable bug.
